target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary
- Cache provider-normalized tool schema `parameters` for repeated embedded agent turns, but only when OpenClaw can identify one of its bundled SDK tool-compat normalizers.
- Keep the cache internal and conservative: bundled DeepSeek, Gemini, and OpenAI compatibility helpers can reuse cloned normalized `parameters`; custom provider `normalizeToolSchemas` hooks bypass the cache.
- Avoid new public provider plugin contracts and avoid new operator-facing runtime flags. Tool objects, execute closures, approval state, guards, and runtime policy are still rebuilt per turn.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: repeated embedded agent turns can avoid redoing bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization when the provider/model/api/schema signature is unchanged. Unknown or custom provider normalizers still run normally and do not participate in the cache.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, current PR head `dee9bc5aa9e603be72a640b3483ae669ade3c29e`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a terminal proof with `node --import tsx --input-type=module` against the production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry. The script used the real bundled OpenAI tool-schema normalizer identity through a provider runtime handle, called normalization twice with the same provider/model/api/schema signature and different `execute` closures, then repeated the same pattern with a custom provider normalizer.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the current head:

```json
{
  "head": "dee9bc5aa9e603be72a640b3483ae669ade3c29e",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 0, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "afterSecond": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 1, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": { "bypass": 2, "hit": 0, "miss": 0, "store": 0, "size": 0 },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: The bundled OpenAI compatibility path produced one miss and store on the first normalization, then one cache hit on the second normalization while preserving the fresh second `execute` closure. The custom provider path bypassed the cache twice and invoked its normalizer twice, proving the cache remains internal to known bundled compatibility helpers.

What was not tested: Full live-provider latency and a remote LLM turn were not tested in this current-head refresh. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache complete tool inventory/materialization, policy resolution, prompt inputs, or full agent-turn startup.

## Additional validation

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/build.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime-errors.test.ts
[test] passed 4 Vitest shards in 10.92s
Test files: 6 passed
Tests: 124 passed

pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed files>
All matched files use the correct format.

git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD && git diff --check
exit 0
```

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Repeated embedded-agent turns reuse bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization for the same provider/model/api/schema signature, while non-bundled/custom provider normalizers still bypass the cache so plugin-specific behavior is not hidden.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout at PR head `b183b3fbe3379f870b8a65e5bf2fe43c8e5aa2ca`; production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry point; real bundled OpenAI normalizer `normalizeOpenAIToolSchemas` via runtime handle; separate custom runtime handle for the non-bundled provider path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran this repo-local Node harness with `node --import tsx --input-type=module`; it imports `./src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts` and `./src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts`, resets the provider tool schema cache, normalizes equivalent OpenAI tool schemas twice, then normalizes a custom provider twice.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "head": "b183b3fbe3379f870b8a65e5bf2fe43c8e5aa2ca",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": {
      "bypass": 0,
      "hit": 0,
      "miss": 1,
      "store": 1,
      "size": 1
    },
    "afterSecond": {
      "bypass": 0,
      "hit": 1,
      "miss": 1,
      "store": 1,
      "size": 1
    },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": {
      "bypass": 2,
      "hit": 0,
      "miss": 0,
      "store": 0,
      "size": 0
    },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The second bundled OpenAI normalization increments cache hits from 0 to 1 and preserves the second tool execute closure, while the custom provider path records two bypasses and zero hits/stores. That proves the cache applies only to bundled compatibility normalization and does not reuse plugin-specific custom output.
- What was not tested: Live external provider latency or a paid remote LLM turn. This proof targets the local embedded-agent normalization/cache behavior directly.

target.title: fix(google): bound TTS success JSON response reads
target.body:
## Origin / follow-up

Follow-up to #96970 and the same bounded-reader fan-out strategy from #96505. #96970 applied the shared provider JSON cap to ClickClack REST success responses; this PR applies the same source-of-truth reader to Google Gemini TTS success JSON responses.

## Summary

- Route Google Gemini TTS success JSON parsing through `readProviderJsonResponse`.
- Keep the existing Google TTS request construction, retry behavior, voice/model selection, and PCM extraction unchanged.
- Add regression coverage for oversized Gemini TTS success JSON stream cancellation.
- Add loopback HTTP proof that exercises real `postJsonRequest`, real `fetch`, and real `readProviderJsonResponse` for both a normal small response and an oversized success body.

## Maintainer-ready fields

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Google Gemini TTS success responses crossed the provider HTTP boundary through raw `response.json()` instead of OpenClaw's shared bounded provider JSON reader.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix
- **Root cause:** Root cause source: the Google TTS success-response parser bypassed the canonical provider response-size contract because `synthesizeGoogleTtsPcmOnce()` called raw `await res.json()` after `postJsonRequest` returned a successful Response.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The Google TTS PCM extraction path has one success JSON parse point, so moving that parse point onto `readProviderJsonResponse` restores the response-size invariant before downstream audio payload extraction and retry handling.
- **Real environment tested:** A deterministic loopback HTTP server was exercised through real `postJsonRequest`, real `fetch`, and real `readProviderJsonResponse`. The proof covers a normal small Google TTS-style JSON envelope and an oversized success JSON stream that closes before the full 18 MiB response is sent.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran the Google speech provider Vitest target, ran `pnpm check:test-types`, then ran the loopback proof with `pnpm --dir /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-96984 exec tsx /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-96984-evidence/google-tts-loopback-proof.ts`.
- **Evidence after fix:** The Google speech provider test file passed with 18 tests; typechecking passed; the loopback proof returned `small_status=200`, `small_pcm_bytes=4`, and `oversized_error=Google TTS response: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes`.
- **Observed result after fix:** The small loopback response parsed normally into the existing inline audio payload shape, while the oversized success response failed at the shared 16 MiB cap and the loopback server observed early stream close at 16,908,288 bytes, before the 18 MiB response completed.
- **What was not tested:** External Google account setup, model availability, and hosted speech synthesis semantics remain outside this patch; request construction, endpoint trust validation, model/voice selection, PCM extraction, and audio conversion are unchanged.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** Oversized Google TTS success responses now fail deterministically at the shared provider JSON cap instead of being materialized by native JSON parsing before PCM extraction.
- **What did NOT change:** Google TTS auth headers, trusted base URL validation, request body shape, retry count, model/voice normalization, PCM extraction, WAV wrapping, Opus transcoding behavior, and persisted runtime data formats are unchanged.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The canonical source-of-truth for provider response-size enforcement is `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`; this change moves Google TTS success JSON parsing onto that shared contract before downstream typed response projection.
- **Target test file:** `extensions/google/speech-provider.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** A real `Response` with a `ReadableStream` body larger than 16 MiB is returned to the Google TTS success path; the reader rejects at the cap and cancels the stream on both retry attempts.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** One parse-site change in `synthesizeGoogleTtsPcmOnce()` covers the only Google TTS success JSON path without touching provider configuration, request generation, audio extraction, or retry policy.
- **Risk labels considered:** `merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility`, `behavior-change: oversized-provider-response`, and `auth-provider`.
- **Risk explanation:** The compatibility change is limited to Google TTS success JSON bodies above the shared 16 MiB cap; auth, endpoint trust, schema expectations, and audio conversion behavior are unchanged.
- **Why acceptable:** Normal Gemini TTS JSON envelopes should be far below 16 MiB, and early rejection matches the provider response-body policy used by #96505 and #96970.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for the code change and loopback proof; maintainers still need to explicitly accept the 16 MiB compatibility boundary before merge.
- **Patch quality notes:** Focused three-file diff, no repository evidence files, no unrelated cleanup, RED failure captured raw `response.json()` behavior, GREEN verification covers target behavior and test typechecking, and loopback proof covers the requested HTTP boundary.

## What Problem This Solves

Google TTS already uses the shared provider HTTP request path, but its successful Gemini response body was still parsed with raw native JSON parsing. This left the success response path outside the bounded-reader guardrail used for provider JSON responses elsewhere.

## Root Cause

The Google TTS implementation bounded provider errors through the shared HTTP stack but parsed successful JSON locally with `await res.json()`. That split the response-body contract: unsuccessful responses were normalized through shared helpers, while successful TTS JSON bypassed the shared size cap before audio payload extraction.

## Why it matters / User impact

A too-large Gemini TTS success response can otherwise be buffered by native JSON parsing before OpenClaw has a chance to reject it. The patch makes the failure deterministic and closes the body stream once the shared cap is exceeded.

## What did NOT change

- Google TTS model and voice defaults are unchanged.
- Google trusted base URL validation and request header construction are unchanged.
- PCM extraction, WAV wrapping, Opus transcoding, and retry count are unchanged.
- No new Google-specific response-size constant was introduced.
- No persisted runtime data model or migration path changes were introduced; the helper change is limited to provider test mocks.

## Architecture / source-of-truth check

OpenClaw already owns provider response-size enforcement in `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`. Google TTS now uses that shared reader for successful Gemini JSON responses instead of maintaining a local native JSON parse path.

## Target test file

`extensions/google/speech-provider.test.ts`

## Scenario locked in

The regression returns a real `Response` with a `ReadableStream` body of `16 * 1024 * 1024 + 1` bytes to the Google TTS success path. The `json()` method is a sentinel: the test fails on the old code when the sentinel is called, and passes after the code reads the stream through the bounded reader.

## Evidence

RED observation against the raw `response.json()` implementation:

```text
AssertionError: expected [Function] to throw error including 'Google TTS response: JSON response ex…' but got 'unbounded json reader was used'

Expected: "Google TTS response: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
Received: "unbounded json reader was used"
```

GREEN target test:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  18 passed (18)
```

Test typecheck:

```text
$ pnpm tsgo:test
$ pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
```

## Loopback HTTP proof

The follow-up proof uses a local loopback HTTP server and sends requests through real `postJsonRequest` with real `fetch`, then parses the returned `Response` through real `readProviderJsonResponse`.

The small path returns a Google TTS-style JSON envelope with inline PCM audio data. The oversized path streams an 18 MiB success response and records where the server sees the client close the connection.

Command:

```text
pnpm --dir /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-96984 exec tsx /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-96984-evidence/google-tts-loopback-proof.ts
```

Observed output:

```text
small_status=200
small_mime=audio/L16;codec=pcm;rate=24000
small_pcm_bytes=4
oversized_error=Google TTS response: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes
oversized_closed_bytes=16908288
oversized_exceeded_cap=true
oversized_closed_before_full_response=true
small_requests=1
oversized_requests=1
```

This directly covers the requested loopback proof: normal small response parsing succeeds, the oversized success body fails with the bounded-reader error, and the server observes early close before the full 18 MiB response is sent.

## Real behavior proof

The unit regression exercises the actual Google speech provider code path after `postJsonRequest` returns a successful `Response`. The follow-up loopback proof additionally exercises the real HTTP request boundary through `postJsonRequest`, real `fetch`, and `readProviderJsonResponse`.

```text
Google TTS response: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes
cancelCount = 2
release calls = 2
```

```text
small_status=200
small_pcm_bytes=4
oversized_closed_before_full_response=true
```

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001 — fixed:** Added loopback HTTP proof that shows both normal small Google TTS-style response parsing and oversized success JSON early close through real `postJsonRequest`, real `fetch`, and real `readProviderJsonResponse`.
- **RF-002 — fixed:** The proof now crosses the HTTP boundary through a loopback server instead of stopping at a mocked `postJsonRequest` unit boundary.
- **RF-003 — fixed:** The loopback output includes `small_status=200`, `small_pcm_bytes=4`, `oversized_error=Google TTS response: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes`, and `oversized_closed_before_full_response=true`.
- **RF-004 — maintainer-decision:** Existing Google TTS success JSON bodies above the shared 16 MiB cap will now fail early. This is the intentional hardening boundary of the patch and should be explicitly accepted by maintainers before merge.
- **RF-005 — maintainer-decision:** The remaining non-code item is maintainer acceptance of the compatibility boundary; the contributor-side proof gap has been addressed with the loopback output above.

## External environment disclosure

No external Google account, API credential, or hosted Google TTS synthesis call was used for this follow-up. The completed verification uses the reviewer-requested loopback option and exercises the provider HTTP request path locally through real `postJsonRequest`, real `fetch`, and real `readProviderJsonResponse`.

## Verification

```text
pnpm --dir /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/followup-96970 exec vitest --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-providers.config.ts run extensions/google/speech-provider.test.ts
pnpm --dir /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/followup-96970 check:test-types
pnpm --dir /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-96984 exec tsx /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-96984-evidence/google-tts-loopback-proof.ts
```

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `extensions/google/speech-provider.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** oversized Gemini TTS success JSON response body crosses the 16 MiB cap.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** the test targets the single Google TTS success parse site and verifies both stream cancellation and retry cleanup without changing request construction.

## Merge risk

- **Detected category:** `auth-provider`, because the changed production file is the Google provider implementation.
- **Compatibility:** Existing Google TTS success JSON bodies above the shared 16 MiB cap now fail early instead of being buffered by native JSON parsing; maintainers should explicitly accept this boundary.
- **Unchanged behavior:** Existing auth headers, trusted base URL validation, request payloads, response schema expectations, retry count, and audio conversion behavior are unchanged.
- **Related open PR scan:** Alix-007 open PR comparison found no `extensions/google/speech-provider.ts` overlap; #96874 covers Volcengine/Xiaomi TTS, #96875 covers Vydra speech, and #96620 covers GoogleChat/Azure Speech rather than Google Gemini TTS.
- **Out-of-scope boundary:** This PR does not change Google account setup, provider configuration defaults, endpoint allowlists, model selection, voice selection, speech synthesis semantics, or audio transcoding.

## Risk labels considered

- `merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility`
- `behavior-change: oversized-provider-response`
- `auth-provider` detected and explained above

## Risk explanation

The only intended behavior change is that Google TTS success JSON bodies over the shared provider cap now fail early with a bounded-reader error. Existing valid responses continue into the same `extractGoogleSpeechPcm` path.

## Why acceptable

The patch aligns Google TTS with OpenClaw's shared provider response-body boundary and keeps the change at the only success JSON parse point. The proof covers both the oversized boundary and a normal small response over loopback HTTP, and existing speech-provider tests continue to pass.

## Maintainer-ready confidence

High for contributor-side readiness. The root cause is localized, the diff is small, related open PR overlap was checked, fresh CI is green on commit `9ce6c78521924393bfc72560f5a1a3cb9f6dbf9a`, and loopback HTTP proof now covers the requested real boundary. The remaining merge decision is maintainer acceptance of the 16 MiB compatibility boundary.

## Patch quality notes

- No unrelated formatting, docs, lockfile, or runtime config changes.
- The shared test helper now exports `readProviderJsonResponse` so existing provider tests can exercise production code that imports the shared reader.
- The test verifies the old raw-JSON path failed before the fix and the bounded-reader path cancels streams after the fix.
- The loopback proof verifies small-response parsing and oversized-response early close through the provider HTTP boundary.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

This PR is intentionally separate from #96970: #96970 covers ClickClack REST success JSON reads, while this PR covers Google Gemini TTS success JSON reads. Related open PR scan found no competing Google Gemini TTS bounded-reader PR.

target.title: feat(subagents): forward toolsAllow from sessions_spawn
target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native subagent runs.
- Validate it as a string array, trim entries, drop blank/whitespace-only entries, preserve explicit `[]`, and reject unsupported ACP paths instead of silently ignoring the policy.
- Forward and persist the allowlist through Gateway/session/embedded-attempt surfaces so later native child turns inherit the stored tool policy.
- Normalize Gateway `agent.run` `toolsAllow` before dispatching to the runner, matching the `sessions_spawn` normalization behavior.
- Keep CLI-backed runtime handling aligned with current `main`: runtime `toolsAllow` is forwarded to the CLI harness so the harness can fail closed when it cannot enforce the allowlist.

## Current upstream status
Latest `upstream/main` already includes broad subagent/runtime work and embedded-runner `toolsAllow` support. This PR remains scoped to the missing public/control-plane plumbing for `sessions_spawn.toolsAllow`, plus Gateway normalization for raw `agent.run` inputs.

## Change Type
- [x] Feature

## Scope
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native subagent tools with `toolsAllow`; the policy is applied to the spawned child embedded runtime so the child receives only allowed tool schemas. Gateway `agent.run` also normalizes direct `toolsAllow` input before runner dispatch.

Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw source checkout at current PR head `230b54baa3175fafeb7160922f510b489a6d1887`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, local Gateway on loopback token auth with `gateway.mode=local`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json` through Gateway (not `--local`), and a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server that records every `/v1/responses` request tool schema.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started a local mock OpenAI Responses server from `/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs/mock-openai-toolsallow.cjs`, started OpenClaw Gateway from the same checkout, then ran the parent agent through Gateway:

```text
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=<loopback-proof-token> OPENAI_API_KEY=mock node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json --session-key agent:default:tools-allow-parent --message 'SUBAGENT_TOOLS_ALLOW_PARENT_PROOF: call sessions_spawn exactly once with toolsAllow ["read"].' --timeout 90
```

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the current-head Gateway proof summarizer. The parent initial request had `sessions_spawn`; the spawned child request had only `read`, with `exec`, `sessions_spawn`, and `sessions_yield` absent. The mock log had 3 requests total: parent initial tool call, parent follow-up after child result, and the restricted spawned child request.

```json
{
  "head": "230b54baa3175fafeb7160922f510b489a6d1887",
  "gatewayAgentRunStatus": "ok",
  "gatewayAgentRunId": "8843fa2b-8a41-49d6-aaa4-ae3487155aa5",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "CHILD_DONE",
  "parentTools": [
    "exec",
    "read",
    "sessions_spawn",
    "sessions_yield",
    "subagents"
  ],
  "restrictedChildTools": [
    "read"
  ],
  "restrictedChildHasRead": true,
  "restrictedChildHasExec": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsSpawn": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsYield": false,
  "totalMockRequests": 3,
  "proofDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs"
}
```

Additional current-head verification already run after resolving conflicts:

```text
node scripts/generate-prompt-snapshots.ts --check
prompt snapshot check passed

node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8
oxlint shards passed

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts --testNamePattern 'toolsAllow|timestamp context|canonical Anthropic' --reporter=dot
focused toolsAllow vitest shards passed
```

GitHub checks observed on current head: PR is mergeable/clean and the current `Real behavior proof` workflow check is passing.

Observed result after fix: A real Gateway-backed parent agent run called `sessions_spawn` with `toolsAllow:["read"]`, the child run completed with `CHILD_DONE`, and the actual spawned child model request only advertised `read`. Disallowed parent/session/runtime tools were not present in the spawned child request.

What was not tested: A paid external provider was not used. The proof used a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server to avoid provider cost while exercising the real Gateway, real embedded runner, real `sessions_spawn` tool call, and real child model request construction.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] Prompt snapshot check
- Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`, `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts`, `src/agents/subagent-spawn.model-session.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts`, `src/commands/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts`, `test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/*`.
- Scenario locked in: `toolsAllow` is exposed, normalized, validates string arrays, preserves explicit `[]`, rejects unsupported ACP runtime, forwards through Gateway/session state, reaches embedded runs started by the command runner, is inherited by persisted child sessions, and is forwarded to CLI harnesses for fail-closed handling.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`runtime="subagent"` callers may pass `toolsAllow` to narrow a spawned native subagent's tool set. Defaults are unchanged when omitted. `toolsAllow: []` intentionally runs the child without tools. `runtime="acp"` rejects `toolsAllow` clearly because ACP cannot enforce this embedded-runner allowlist.

## Security Impact
- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

Risk + mitigation: this only narrows the child tool set for native subagent runs. Unsupported ACP paths reject the option so callers do not get a false sense that an allowlist was enforced. CLI-backed runs receive the runtime allowlist at the harness boundary so the harness can fail closed when enforcement is unavailable.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect ACP runtime spawns.
  - Mitigation: the tool rejects `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` with a clear input error.
- Risk: callers may expect all CLI harnesses to enforce runtime allowlists.
  - Mitigation: OpenClaw forwards the allowlist to the CLI harness boundary for fail-closed handling instead of silently dropping it.
- Risk: public API naming/semantics need maintainer judgment.
  - Mitigation: the implementation is additive and reuses existing embedded-runner allowlist semantics rather than adding a second filtering mechanism.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native child tools with `toolsAllow`; the spawned child embedded model request advertises only the allowed tools instead of inheriting the full parent native tool set.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout at PR head `99c0bdd3cb556a7a407c0ae45e0931fa82617dc3`; temporary OpenClaw state/config; real Gateway loopback token auth; real `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run`; real `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json`; deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server logging `/v1/responses` requests.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a repo-local Node proof harness that created temp state/config, started the mock OpenAI-compatible server, started the real Gateway, then invoked `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json --session-key agent:default:tools-allow-parent --message 'SUBAGENT_TOOLS_ALLOW_PARENT_PROOF: call sessions_spawn exactly once with toolsAllow ["read"].' --timeout 120` with the temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.

- Observed result after fix: The parent request still exposes the full native tool set and calls `sessions_spawn` once, while the spawned child request exposes only `["read"]`; it has `read` and does not have `exec`, `sessions_spawn`, or `sessions_yield`. The real Gateway agent run exits 0 with status `ok`.

target.title: fix(agents): detect peer/conversation IDs from --bind values via channel plugin hook
target.body:
### What Problem This Solves

`agents add --bind "feishu:oc_xxx"` stores the value after the colon in `match.accountId`. For Feishu, IDs starting with `oc_` are chat/group identifiers (`chat_id`), not channel account IDs. The routing engine matches conversations using the `peer` field, so the binding was invisible to the router and messages fell through to the default agent.

### Evidence

**Fix:** Added an optional `resolveBindingPeerFromAccountId` hook to the channel plugin setup adapter. When a plugin recognizes the explicit `--bind` value as a peer/conversation ID, it is stored in `match.peer` instead of `match.accountId`.

**Feishu implementation:**
| ID prefix | Type | Peer result |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| `oc_*` | Group chat_id | `{ kind: "group", id }` |
| `ou_*` | User open_id | `{ kind: "direct", id }` |
| other | Unknown | Falls back to accountId (existing behavior) |

**Test matrix:**
| Scenario | `--bind` value | Before (accountId) | After (peer) |
|----------|---------------|-------------------|--------------|
| Feishu group | `feishu:oc_xxx` | `{ accountId: "oc_xxx" }` ❌ | `{ peer: { kind:"group", id:"oc_xxx" } }` ✅ |
| Feishu DM | `feishu:ou_xxx` | `{ accountId: "ou_xxx" }` ❌ | `{ peer: { kind:"direct", id:"ou_xxx" } }` ✅ |
| Other channel | `telegram:default` | `{ accountId: "default" }` ✅ (unchanged) | `{ accountId: "default" }` ✅ |

**Files changed:** 3 files, +70 / -12 lines
- `src/channels/plugins/types.adapters.ts` — New hook type
- `extensions/feishu/src/setup-core.ts` — Feishu implementation
- `src/commands/agents.bindings.ts` — `parseBindingSpecs()` + `buildChannelBindings()` use the hook

**Format check:** All matched files use the correct format.

target.title: Fix issue#95927:Install bug:No matching version found for node-domexception@1.0.28
target.body: Closes #95927 ## What Problem This Solves Fixes an issue(https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/95927) where users cloning and installing OpenClaw from source would encounter a hard pnpm install failure due to an unresolvable node-domexception@1.0.28 dependency version, completely blocking the build and development workflow. This error is triggered when downloaded extensions include plugins that depend on gaxios (such as the Google Chat plugin), as this introduces gaxios@7.1.5 and pulls in the node-domexception sub-dependency chain for the first time. ## Why This Change Was Made Adds a _node-domexception:1.0.0_ override to the workspace root so that pnpm resolves the dependency to the existing 1.0.0 release, aligning with the 1.0.0 constraint already declared by fetch-blob. This fixes resolution failures triggered by gaxios-based plugins without upgrading fetch-blob or node-fetch, as both remain tied to the node-domexception 1.x line for CommonJS compatibility. Add _'@napi-rs/wasm-runtime@1.1.6'_ in _./pnpm-workspace.yaml.minimumReleaseAgeExclude_ to avoid the follow error: `pnpm install Scope: all 156 workspace projects ✗ Lockfile failed supply-chain policy check (1361 entries in 17.3s) [ERR_PNPM_MINIMUM_RELEASE_AGE_VIOLATION] 1 lockfile entries failed verification: @napi-rs/wasm-runtime@1.1.6 was published at 2026-06-24T07:22:04.062Z, within the minimumReleaseAge cutoff (2026-06-24T05:35:32.681Z) ` This error both happened on my machine and remote CI. ## User Impact Users can now successfully install and build OpenClaw from source even when their selected extensions include plugins that depend on gaxios (such as the Google Chat plugin). Previously, this workflow would fail with an ERR_PNPM_NO_MATCHING_VERSION error when downloaded extensions include such plugins, blocking the build entirely; after this fix, pnpm install && pnpm build && pnpm ui:build completes cleanly regardless of such plugins are enabled. ## Evidence before fixing: after fixing: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile: pnpm: build: pnpm: ui build: ## Root Cause: Unresolvable node-domexception Dependency ## Error Scope: all 153 workspace projects ✓ Lockfile passes supply-chain policies (1360 entries in 17s) [ERR_PNPM_NO_MATCHING_VERSION] No matching version found for node-domexception@1.0.28 while fetching it from https://registry.npmjs.org/ This error happened while installing the dependencies of @google/genai@2.7.0 at google-auth-library@10.7.0 at gaxios@7.1.5 at node-fetch@3.3.2 at fetch-blob@3.2.0 The latest release of node-domexception is "2.0.2". ## History Issue https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/60338 was previously fixed by pinning node-domexception to 1.0.28 in package.json. That version is no longer available on the npm registry. The package has since been deprecated by its author; only four versions remain: 1.0.0, 2.0.0, 2.0.1, and 2.0.2. ## Trigger The error appears when downloaded extensions include plugins that depend on gaxios—such as the Google Chat plugin. The first install succeeds without these plugins. The second install pulls in gaxios@7.1.5, which introduces the node-fetch → fetch-blob → node-domexception chain for the first time. ## Why Downgrade Is Not Viable fetch-blob@3.1.4 introduced the node-domexception requirement to fix a critical double-slice bug for filesystem-backed blobs. node-fetch@3.3.2 depends on fetch-blob@^3.1.4 specifically to include this fix. Downgrading either package would reintroduce the bug. ## The Fix fetch-blob itself declares node-domexception: ^1.0.0. While 2.0.2 appears to work in local testing, it is ESM-only and may introduce compatibility issues with the CommonJS-based fetch-blob 3.x line. The safest resolution is to add node-domexception: 1.0.0 to pnpm-workspace.yaml, aligning with fetch-blob's own constraint. The package.json override was already removed from main in favor of workspace-level configuration, so pnpm-workspace.yaml is the correct location for this fix. ## For the Review The errors reported by ```Dependency Guard / dependency-guard (pull_request_target) ``` and ```CI / checks-node-compact-small-4 (pull_request)``` are triggered by the modification of the node-domexception version in this PR, and do not reflect actual critical problems. I'm also puzzled by the ```CI / check-shrinkwrap (pull_request) ``` failure. The command node scripts/generate-npm-shrinkwrap.mjs --all --check passes locally on my machine, but after pushing to the PR branch, GitHub CI fails with: `plain Run set -euo pipefail $ node scripts/generate-npm-shrinkwrap.mjs --all --check generated npm-shrinkwrap.json violates workspace overrides: node_modules/cliui locked 8.0.1, expected 6.0.0; node_modules/wrap-ansi locked 7.0.0, expected 6.2.0; node_modules/y18n locked 5.0.8, expected 4.0.3; node_modules/yargs-parser locked 21.1.1, expected 18.1.3 [ELIFECYCLE] Command failed with exit code 1. Error: Process completed with exit code 1.` And here is my pass evidence on my machine: I've spent several hours on this, including aligning pnpm versions and double-checking these dependency versions, but I'm still at a loss. I'll try to find some free time (if any) to keep investigating, but I'd greatly appreciate any insights or assistance from maintainers or other contributors. Thanks in advance!

## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native subagent runs.
- Validate it as a string array, trim entries, drop blank/whitespace-only entries, preserve explicit `[]`, and reject unsupported ACP paths instead of silently ignoring the policy.
- Forward and persist the allowlist through Gateway/session/embedded-attempt surfaces so later native child turns inherit the stored tool policy.
- Normalize Gateway `agent.run` `toolsAllow` before dispatching to the runner, matching the `sessions_spawn` normalization behavior.
- Keep CLI-backed runtime handling aligned with current `main`: runtime `toolsAllow` is forwarded to the CLI harness so the harness can fail closed when it cannot enforce the allowlist.

## Current upstream status
Latest `upstream/main` already includes broad subagent/runtime work and embedded-runner `toolsAllow` support. This PR remains scoped to the missing public/control-plane plumbing for `sessions_spawn.toolsAllow`, plus Gateway normalization for raw `agent.run` inputs.

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR

target.title: fix(ui): show friendly session identifiers instead of raw open_id/session keys
target.body:
Fixes #61213
  
  ## What Problem This Solves
  
  The Web Control UI displays raw machine-generated identifiers in session
  labels, making it impossible for operators to quickly identify sessions:
  
  - Activity log shows `agent:main:feishu:direct:ou_67075ec667cac0a7feae2c5094fd27b2`
    instead of a readable label like `Feishu · ou_67075ec66…`
  - Exec approval dialogs show the same raw session keys
  - Chinese platforms (Feishu, QQ, DingTalk, WeChat, WeCom) were missing from
    CHANNEL_LABELS, so even the fallback display was unhelpful
  
  The proposed fix priority from #61213: binding.comment → agent.name →
  technical identifier. This PR handles the UI-side display improvements.
  Server-side binding.comment propagation will be a follow-up.
  
  ## Why This Change Was Made
  
  - `CHANNEL_LABELS` only had 9 Western platforms; 18 channel labels added
  - `activity.ts` and `exec-approval.ts` rendered `sessionKey` raw without any
    display resolution — they now use `parseSessionKey()` for friendly fallback
  - Long machine-generated identifiers (>20 chars, like `ou_` open_ids and
    UUIDs) now truncate to keep the UI compact, with the full key preserved
    via the `title` attribute on hover
  
  ## User Impact
  
  | Before | After |
  |--------|-------|
  | `agent:main:feishu:direct:ou_67075ec667cac0a7feae2c5094fd27b2` | `Feishu · ou_67075ec66…` |
  | `agent:main:qqbot:direct:123456` | `QQ · 123456` |
  | `agent:main:wechat:direct:wxid_abc123` | `WeChat · wxid_abc123` |
  | Raw session key in activity log | Formatted with parseSessionKey() |
  | Raw session key in exec approval | Formatted with formatSessionKey() |
  
  ## Evidence
  
  $ pnpm test ui/src/ui/session-display.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts
  
   Test Files  2 passed (2)
        Tests  108 passed (108)
  
  - 33 new unit tests covering all 18 new channels, truncation edge cases,
    and resolveSessionDisplayName fallback chain
  - 75 existing app-render.helpers tests pass with no regressions
  - Format check (`pnpm format:check`) passes on all 4 changed files
  
  ---

target.title: fix(google): bound TTS success JSON response reads
target.body:
## Origin / follow-up

Follow-up to #96970 and the same bounded-reader fan-out strategy from #96505. #96970 applied the shared provider JSON cap to ClickClack REST success responses; this PR applies the same source-of-truth reader to Google Gemini TTS success JSON responses.

## Summary

- Route Google Gemini TTS success JSON parsing through `readProviderJsonResponse`.
- Keep the existing Google TTS request construction, retry behavior, voice/model selection, and PCM extraction unchanged.
- Add regression coverage for oversized Gemini TTS success JSON stream cancellation.
- Add loopback HTTP proof that exercises real `postJsonRequest`, real `fetch`, and real `readProviderJsonResponse` for both a normal small response and an oversized success body.

## Maintainer-ready fields

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Google Gemini TTS success responses crossed the provider HTTP boundary through raw `response.json()` instead of OpenClaw's shared bounded provider JSON reader.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix
- **Root cause:** Root cause source: the Google TTS success-response parser bypassed the canonical provider response-size contract because `synthesizeGoogleTtsPcmOnce()` called raw `await res.json()` after `postJsonRequest` returned a successful Response.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The Google TTS PCM extraction path has one success JSON parse point, so moving that parse point onto `readProviderJsonResponse` restores the response-size invariant before downstream audio payload extraction and retry handling.
- **Real environment tested:** A deterministic loopback HTTP server was exercised through real `postJsonRequest`, real `fetch`, and real `readProviderJsonResponse`. The proof covers a normal small Google TTS-style JSON envelope and an oversized success JSON stream that closes before the full 18 MiB response is sent.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran the Google speech provider Vitest target, ran `pnpm check:test-types`, then ran the loopback proof with `pnpm --dir /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-96984 exec tsx /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-96984-evidence/google-tts-loopback-proof.ts`.
- **Evidence after fix:** The Google speech provider test file passed with 18 tests; typechecking passed; the loopback proof returned `small_status=200`, `small_pcm_bytes=4`, and `oversized_error=Google TTS response: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes`.
- **Observed result after fix:** The small loopback response parsed normally into the existing inline audio payload shape, while the oversized success response failed at the shared 16 MiB cap and the loopback server observed early stream close at 16,908,288 bytes, before the 18 MiB response completed.
- **What was not tested:** External Google account setup, model availability, and hosted speech synthesis semantics remain outside this patch; request construction, endpoint trust validation, model/voice selection, PCM extraction, and audio conversion are unchanged.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** Oversized Google TTS success responses now fail deterministically at the shared provider JSON cap instead of being materialized by native JSON parsing before PCM extraction.
- **What did NOT change:** Google TTS auth headers, trusted base URL validation, request body shape, retry count, model/voice normalization, PCM extraction, WAV wrapping, Opus transcoding behavior, and persisted runtime data formats are unchanged.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The canonical source-of-truth for provider response-size enforcement is `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`; this change moves Google TTS success JSON parsing onto that shared contract before downstream typed response projection.
- **Target test file:** `extensions/google/speech-provider.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** A real `Response` with a `ReadableStream` body larger than 16 MiB is returned to the Google TTS success path; the reader rejects at the cap and cancels the stream on both retry attempts.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** One parse-site change in `synthesizeGoogleTtsPcmOnce()` covers the only Google TTS success JSON path without touching provider configuration, request generation, audio extraction, or retry policy.
- **Risk labels considered:** `merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility`, `behavior-change: oversized-provider-response`, and `auth-provider`.
- **Risk explanation:** The compatibility change is limited to Google TTS success JSON bodies above the shared 16 MiB cap; auth, endpoint trust, schema expectations, and audio conversion behavior are unchanged.
- **Why acceptable:** Normal Gemini TTS JSON envelopes should be far below 16 MiB, and early rejection matches the provider response-body policy used by #96505 and #96970.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for the code change and loopback proof; maintainers still need to explicitly accept the 16 MiB compatibility boundary before merge.
- **Patch quality notes:** Focused three-file diff, no repository evidence files, no unrelated cleanup, RED failure captured raw `response.json()` behavior, GREEN verification covers target behavior and test typechecking, and loopback proof covers the requested HTTP boundary.

## What Problem This Solves

Google TTS already uses the shared provider HTTP request path, but its successful Gemini response body was still parsed with raw native JSON parsing. This left the success response path outside the bounded-reader guardrail used for provider JSON responses elsewhere.

## Root Cause

The Google TTS implementation bounded provider errors through the shared HTTP stack but parsed successful JSON locally with `await res.json()`. That split the response-body contract: unsuccessful responses were normalized through shared helpers, while successful TTS JSON bypassed the shared size cap before audio payload extraction.

## Why it matters / User impact

A too-large Gemini TTS success response can otherwise be buffered by native JSON parsing before OpenClaw has a chance to reject it. The patch makes the failure deterministic and closes the body stream once the shared cap is exceeded.

## What did NOT change

- Google TTS model and voice defaults are unchanged.
- Google trusted base URL validation and request header construction are unchanged.
- PCM extraction, WAV wrapping, Opus transcoding, and retry count are unchanged.
- No new Google-specific response-size constant was introduced.
- No persisted runtime data model or migration path changes were introduced; the helper change is limited to provider test mocks.

## Architecture / source-of-truth check

OpenClaw already owns provider response-size enforcement in `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`. Google TTS now uses that shared reader for successful Gemini JSON responses instead of maintaining a local native JSON parse path.

## Target test file

`extensions/google/speech-provider.test.ts`

## Scenario locked in

The regression returns a real `Response` with a `ReadableStream` body of `16 * 1024 * 1024 + 1` bytes to the Google TTS success path. The `json()` method is a sentinel: the test fails on the old code when the sentinel is called, and passes after the code reads the stream through the bounded reader.

## Evidence

RED observation against the raw `response.json()` implementation:

```text
AssertionError: expected [Function] to throw error including 'Google TTS response: JSON response ex…' but got 'unbounded json reader was used'

Expected: "Google TTS response: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
Received: "unbounded json reader was used"
```

GREEN target test:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  18 passed (18)
```

Test typecheck:

```text
$ pnpm tsgo:test
$ pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
```

## Loopback HTTP proof

The follow-up proof uses a local loopback HTTP server and sends requests through real `postJsonRequest` with real `fetch`, then parses the returned `Response` through real `readProviderJsonResponse`.

The small path returns a Google TTS-style JSON envelope with inline PCM audio data. The oversized path streams an 18 MiB success response and records where the server sees the client close the connection.

Command:

```text
pnpm --dir /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-96984 exec tsx /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-96984-evidence/google-tts-loopback-proof.ts
```

Observed output:

```text
small_status=200
small_mime=audio/L16;codec=pcm;rate=24000
small_pcm_bytes=4
oversized_error=Google TTS response: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes
oversized_closed_bytes=16908288
oversized_exceeded_cap=true
oversized_closed_before_full_response=true
small_requests=1
oversized_requests=1
```

This directly covers the requested loopback proof: normal small response parsing succeeds, the oversized success body fails with the bounded-reader error, and the server observes early close before the full 18 MiB response is sent.

## Real behavior proof

The unit regression exercises the actual Google speech provider code path after `postJsonRequest` returns a successful `Response`. The follow-up loopback proof additionally exercises the real HTTP request boundary through `postJsonRequest`, real `fetch`, and `readProviderJsonResponse`.

```text
Google TTS response: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes
cancelCount = 2
release calls = 2
```

```text
small_status=200
small_pcm_bytes=4
oversized_closed_before_full_response=true
```

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001 — fixed:** Added loopback HTTP proof that shows both normal small Google TTS-style response parsing and oversized success JSON early close through real `postJsonRequest`, real `fetch`, and real `readProviderJsonResponse`.
- **RF-002 — fixed:** The proof now crosses the HTTP boundary through a loopback server instead of stopping at a mocked `postJsonRequest` unit boundary.
- **RF-003 — fixed:** The loopback output includes `small_status=200`, `small_pcm_bytes=4`, `oversized_error=Google TTS response: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes`, and `oversized_closed_before_full_response=true`.
- **RF-004 — maintainer-decision:** Existing Google TTS success JSON bodies above the shared 16 MiB cap will now fail early. This is the intentional hardening boundary of the patch and should be explicitly accepted by maintainers before merge.
- **RF-005 — maintainer-decision:** The remaining non-code item is maintainer acceptance of the compatibility boundary; the contributor-side proof gap has been addressed with the loopback output above.

## External environment disclosure

No external Google account, API credential, or hosted Google TTS synthesis call was used for this follow-up. The completed verification uses the reviewer-requested loopback option and exercises the provider HTTP request path locally through real `postJsonRequest`, real `fetch`, and real `readProviderJsonResponse`.

## Verification

```text
pnpm --dir /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/followup-96970 exec vitest --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-providers.config.ts run extensions/google/speech-provider.test.ts
pnpm --dir /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/followup-96970 check:test-types
pnpm --dir /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-96984 exec tsx /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-96984-evidence/google-tts-loopback-proof.ts
```

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `extensions/google/speech-provider.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** oversized Gemini TTS success JSON response body crosses the 16 MiB cap.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** the test targets the single Google TTS success parse site and verifies both stream cancellation and retry cleanup without changing request construction.

## Merge risk

- **Detected category:** `auth-provider`, because the changed production file is the Google provider implementation.
- **Compatibility:** Existing Google TTS success JSON bodies above the shared 16 MiB cap now fail early instead of being buffered by native JSON parsing; maintainers should explicitly accept this boundary.
- **Unchanged behavior:** Existing auth headers, trusted base URL validation, request payloads, response schema expectations, retry count, and audio conversion behavior are unchanged.
- **Related open PR scan:** Alix-007 open PR comparison found no `extensions/google/speech-provider.ts` overlap; #96874 covers Volcengine/Xiaomi TTS, #96875 covers Vydra speech, and #96620 covers GoogleChat/Azure Speech rather than Google Gemini TTS.
- **Out-of-scope boundary:** This PR does not change Google account setup, provider configuration defaults, endpoint allowlists, model selection, voice selection, speech synthesis semantics, or audio transcoding.

## Risk labels considered

- `merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility`
- `behavior-change: oversized-provider-response`
- `auth-provider` detected and explained above

## Risk explanation

The only intended behavior change is that Google TTS success JSON bodies over the shared provider cap now fail early with a bounded-reader error. Existing valid responses continue into the same `extractGoogleSpeechPcm` path.

## Why acceptable

The patch aligns Google TTS with OpenClaw's shared provider response-body boundary and keeps the change at the only success JSON parse point. The proof covers both the oversized boundary and a normal small response over loopback HTTP, and existing speech-provider tests continue to pass.

## Maintainer-ready confidence

High for contributor-side readiness. The root cause is localized, the diff is small, related open PR overlap was checked, fresh CI is green on commit `9ce6c78521924393bfc72560f5a1a3cb9f6dbf9a`, and loopback HTTP proof now covers the requested real boundary. The remaining merge decision is maintainer acceptance of the 16 MiB compatibility boundary.

## Patch quality notes

- No unrelated formatting, docs, lockfile, or runtime config changes.
- The shared test helper now exports `readProviderJsonResponse` so existing provider tests can exercise production code that imports the shared reader.
- The test verifies the old raw-JSON path failed before the fix and the bounded-reader path cancels streams after the fix.
- The loopback proof verifies small-response parsing and oversized-response early close through the provider HTTP boundary.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

This PR is intentionally separate from #96970: #96970 covers ClickClack REST success JSON reads, while this PR covers Google Gemini TTS success JSON reads. Related open PR scan found no competing Google Gemini TTS bounded-reader PR.

target.title: fix(provider-transport-fetch): bound SSE buffer to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.ts` has two unbounded memory accumulation paths in `sanitizeOpenAISdkSseResponse`:

1. **SSE sanitizer** (`enqueueSanitized`, line 174): Accumulates decoded chunks into a `buffer` string that only drains when a `\n\n` SSE event boundary is found. If data arrives without boundaries, the buffer grows unboundedly — the same OOM vector fixed for the OpenAI `parseSSE` in #96972.

2. **JSON-to-SSE synthesis** (line 134): Accumulates an entire streaming JSON body into `buffer` inside a `for(;;)` read loop. A hostile or misconfigured endpoint streaming JSON without Content-Length can cause the buffer to grow without bound, exhausting process memory.

These affect every non-OpenAI provider call routed through the guarded model fetch path (OpenRouter, custom OpenAI-compatible endpoints, and proxies).

## Changes

- `src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.ts` (+23 LoC): Added two constants:
  - `SSE_SANITIZE_BUFFER_MAX_BYTES = 64 KiB` — caps the internal SSE sanitizer buffer between event boundaries, matching the `parseSSE` fix in #96972.
  - `SSE_SYNTHESIZE_JSON_MAX_BYTES = 16 MiB` — caps total bytes accumulated when synthesizing a streaming JSON body into SSE frames, matching the existing `readResponseTextLimited` default.

  After each chunk decode in the sanitizer (`enqueueSanitized`), a `buffer.length` check throws if the buffer exceeds 64 KiB without finding an event boundary. After each chunk in the JSON synthesis loop, a `totalBytes` check throws if the accumulated body exceeds 16 MiB.

- `src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.test.ts` (+96 LoC): Added inline tests:
  - `"errors on oversized SSE body without event boundary in sanitizer"` — 65 KiB SSE without `\n\n` → buffer cap error.
  - `"errors on oversized streaming JSON body without content-length in SSE synthesis"` — 17 MiB streaming JSON → synthesis cap error.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Two unbounded memory accumulation vectors in the provider transport SSE sanitization and JSON-to-SSE synthesis paths, both of which could OOM the gateway when a hostile or misconfigured endpoint streams data without expected delimiters or Content-Length.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64, Node 22.19, OpenClaw main@9a735bea03
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node --import tsx _proof_transport_fetch.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  PASS  sanitizer: oversized SSE body throws buffer cap error (server sent 65.0 KiB, cap=64.0 KiB)
  PASS  sanitizer: valid SSE with \n\n boundaries passes through (server sent 19 B)
  PASS  synthesis: oversized streaming JSON body throws cap error (server sent 17.0 MiB, cap=16.0 MiB)
  PASS  synthesis: small valid JSON body accumulates normally (server sent 11 B)
  PASS  negative control: unbounded read would accumulate full 17.0 MiB (OOM vector confirmed)

  Results: 5/5 passed
  ```
  75/75 unit tests passed: `pnpm test src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.test.ts --run`
- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Real `node:http` server delivering 65 KiB SSE without `\n\n` → sanitizer throws buffer cap error at 64 KiB.
  - Real server delivering valid SSE with `\n\n` → passes through correctly.
  - Real server streaming 17 MiB of JSON without Content-Length → synthesis throws cap error at 16 MiB.
  - Real server delivering small valid JSON → accumulates and passes through normally.
  - Negative control: without the cap, the same 17 MiB JSON body is fully accumulated in memory (confirmed OOM vector).
- **What was not tested**: OpenAI native provider path (official `api.openai.com` uses WebSocket, not SSE sanitization); extension-level providers; the `classifyOpenAISdkStreamBody` sniff path (bounded at 2 KiB by `OPENAI_SDK_STREAM_CONTENT_SNIFF_BYTES`).

## Out of scope

- The `classifyOpenAISdkStreamBody` sniff function is already bounded by `OPENAI_SDK_STREAM_CONTENT_SNIFF_BYTES` (2 KiB) and is not affected.
- The non-streaming `readResponseTextLimited` / `readProviderJsonResponse` paths were bounded in earlier Alix-007 PRs (e.g., #96768, #96762).

## Risk

**Low.** Both caps (64 KiB and 16 MiB) are well above any reasonable event payload or response body. If triggered, the stream errors with a clear message rather than silently terminating. The change is additive — no existing behavior is modified, only guards added. Both constants are file-local.

AI-assisted; reviewed before submission.

target.title: fix(openai): bound SSE parser buffer to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The `parseSSE` generator in `src/llm/providers/openai-chatgpt-responses.ts:719` accumulates decoded SSE chunks into an internal `buffer` string (line 726) that only shrinks when a `\n\n` event boundary is found. If a hostile or misconfigured SSE endpoint sends data without double-newline delimiters, the buffer grows unboundedly — consuming all available memory and OOM-killing the gateway process.

This affects every streaming Codex/OpenAI chat completion call routed through the SSE transport path (`transport: "sse"` or WebSocket failure fallback).

## Changes

- `src/llm/providers/openai-chatgpt-responses.ts` (+10 LoC): Added `SSE_PARSE_MAX_BUFFER_BYTES = 64 KiB` constant. After each chunk decode, the buffer check throws `CodexProtocolError` if the accumulated buffer exceeds the cap — preventing unbounded memory growth.

- `src/llm/providers/openai-chatgpt-responses.test.ts` (+35 LoC): Added inline test `"rejects oversized SSE response that exceeds buffer cap"` — creates a `Response` with a `ReadableStream` body of 65 KiB (no `\n\n`), runs it through the production `streamOpenAICodexResponses` -> `parseSSE` chain, and asserts the error shows the buffer cap message.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Unbounded SSE buffer growth when streaming endpoint sends data without \n\n event delimiters — buffer capped at 64 KiB, throws CodexProtocolError on overflow.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64, Node 22.19, OpenClaw main@81e53202f2
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  node --import tsx _proof_sse_cap.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  PASS  oversized body: real fetch throws buffer cap error (server sent 65.0 KiB, cap=64.0 KiB)
  PASS  well-formed SSE: parsed 1 event(s) correctly (server sent 71 B)
  PASS  exact-cap body: accumulated 64.0 KiB without throwing (cap is strict >, not >=)
  PASS  negative control: unbounded read accumulates 65.0 KiB past the 64.0 KiB cap (without fix, OOM vector confirmed)

  Results: 4/4 passed
  ```
  17/17 unit tests passed: `pnpm test src/llm/providers/openai-chatgpt-responses.test.ts --run`
- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Real `node:http` server on 127.0.0.1 delivered a 65 KiB SSE body without `\n\n` → client threw `CodexProtocolError: Codex SSE response exceeded max buffer size (65536 bytes)`. Server-side bytes-sent: 65.0 KiB.
  - Same server with well-formed SSE (71 B including `\n\n`) → parsed 1 event correctly.
  - Exact-cap boundary (64 KiB, no `\n\n`) → accumulated without throwing (cap uses strict `>`, not `>=`).
  - Negative control: same 65 KiB body without the cap check → full 65 KiB accumulated in-memory (OOM vector confirmed).
- **What was not tested**: WebSocket transport path (different parser); non-OpenAI providers (separate PRs for Anthropic, agent transport streams).

## Out of scope

- Anthropic SSE decoder (`src/llm/providers/anthropic.ts:345` — `iterateSseMessages`) has the same pattern plus `data[]`/`raw[]` array accumulation. Follow-up PR.
- Agent transport SSE buffers (`src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.ts`, `src/agents/runtime/proxy.ts`) — separate PRs.

## Risk

**Low.** The cap (64 KiB) is well above any reasonable SSE event payload. If triggered, the stream errors with a clear `CodexProtocolError` message rather than silently terminating. The change is additive — no existing behavior is modified, only a guard added.

AI-assisted; reviewed before submission.

target.title: fix(proxy): bound SSE parser buffer to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The `streamProxy` SSE reader in `src/agents/runtime/proxy.ts:231` accumulates decoded chunks into an internal `buffer` string that only shrinks when a `\n` line boundary is found. If a hostile or misconfigured proxy endpoint sends data without newline delimiters, the buffer grows unboundedly — the same OOM vector fixed for the OpenAI `parseSSE` in #96972.

This affects every LLM call routed through the OpenClaw runtime proxy gateway.

## Changes

- `src/agents/runtime/proxy.ts` (+10 LoC): Added `PROXY_SSE_BUFFER_MAX_BYTES = 64 KiB` constant. After each chunk decode, the buffer check throws an error if the accumulated buffer exceeds the cap — preventing unbounded memory growth. Mirrors the `parseSSE` fix in #96972 exactly.

- `src/agents/runtime/proxy.test.ts` (+27 LoC): Added inline test `"rejects oversized SSE response without line boundary in proxy reader"` — 65 KiB proxy SSE response without `\n` → buffer cap error.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Unbounded SSE buffer growth in runtime proxy reader when data arrives without \n delimiters — buffer capped at 64 KiB.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64, Node 22.19, OpenClaw main@ec737ee74d
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node --import tsx _proof_proxy_cap.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  PASS  oversized body: buffer cap error (server sent 65.0 KiB, cap=64.0 KiB)
  PASS  valid SSE: data passes through (server sent 39 B, 1 line(s))
  PASS  negative control: unbounded read buffer = 65.0 KiB (server sent 65.0 KiB, cap=64.0 KiB)

  Results: 3/3 passed
  ```
  5/5 unit tests passed: `pnpm test src/agents/runtime/proxy.test.ts --run`
- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Real `node:http` server delivering 65 KiB SSE without `\n` → reader throws buffer cap error at 64 KiB. Server-side bytes-sent: 65.0 KiB.
  - Same server with valid SSE data (39 B, `data: {"type":"done","reason":"stop"}\n\n`) → passes through correctly.
  - Negative control: same 65 KiB body without the cap → full 65 KiB accumulated in-memory (OOM vector confirmed).
- **What was not tested**: The proxy WebSocket path (uses a different parser); non-streaming proxy calls.

## Out of scope

- This is the parser-buffer counterpart to the stream-level cap in #96768 (16 MiB via `createSseByteGuard`). That PR and this one are complementary — #96768 caps total bytes, this PR caps the per-frame accumulation.

## Risk

**Low.** The cap (64 KiB) is well above any reasonable proxy event payload. If triggered, the stream errors with a clear message rather than silently terminating. The change is additive — no existing behavior is modified, only a guard added.

target.title: fix(auth): auto-correct type:token to type:api_key for Anthropic Enterprise API keys
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When a user configures an Anthropic Enterprise API key with type: token (instead of type: api_key), the auth profile loader silently rejects it. The Enterprise API uses x-api-key header authentication — the same mechanism as standard API keys — so treating type: token as type: api_key is a safe auto-fix.

## Changes

src/agents/auth-profiles/persisted.ts: Add auto-correction mapping that converts type: token to type: api_key for Anthropic Enterprise API profiles.

## Evidence

Auth profile loading tests pass. The correction only affects Anthropic Enterprise profiles.

**Label**: bug
_AI-assisted._

target.title: fix(opencode-go): treat block-boundary SSE events as liveness for idle timer
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenCode Go sends block-boundary SSE events (text_end, thinking_end, toolcall_start, toolcall_end) between content blocks. The stalled-stream liveness wrapper only recognized text_delta/thinking_delta/toolcall_delta as progress, causing premature timeouts on slow-but-live connections.

## Changes

`extensions/opencode-go/stream-termination.ts`: Add 4 event types to the liveness predicate.

`extensions/opencode-go/stream-termination.test.ts`: Add regression test verifying all 4 boundary events reset the idle timer.

## Evidence

```
pnpm exec vitest run extensions/opencode-go/
  Test Files  5 passed (5)
       Tests  28 passed (28)
```

**Label**: bug

target.title: fix: support baseURL alias in memory embedding provider config resolution
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When users configure `models.providers.openai.baseURL` (camelCase with capital R) pointing to a local Ollama or OpenAI-compatible endpoint, `ModelProviderSchema` silently drops the `baseURL` key via `.strict()`. Memory embeddings then fall through to the built-in OpenAI adapter and route to `api.openai.com/v1` instead of the configured local endpoint.

Fixes #93396

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts:495-522` | Accept `baseURL` as optional field + normalize to `baseUrl` in `superRefine`. Downstream code only reads `baseUrl` — no changes needed. URL redaction is auto-fixed (covers `baseUrl` paths). |
| `src/agents/memory-search.ts:182-185` | Skip built-in adapter when provider has custom `baseUrl`. Before: a `providerId === "openai"` match returned the direct adapter unconditionally. After: checks `providerConfig.baseUrl` and skips when configured. |
| `src/config/schema.test.ts` | +2 tests: normalization of `baseURL` → `baseUrl`, and `baseUrl` priority over `baseURL` |
| `src/agents/memory-search.test.ts` | +2 tests: skip adapter with custom baseUrl, use adapter without one |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Fix for issue #93396 — `models.providers.*.baseURL` is accepted by `.strict()` and normalized to canonical `baseUrl`, routing memory embeddings to the configured endpoint instead of `api.openai.com/v1`.

**Real setup tested**: Node.js v24.13.1 on Linux x86_64

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:
```bash
# 1. Schema normalization — real production code path
node --import tsx /tmp/verify-baseurl-normalize.mjs

# 2. Tests
cd $WORKTREE
pnpm test src/agents/memory-search.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
$ node --import tsx /tmp/verify-baseurl-normalize.mjs

=== Test 1: baseURL → baseUrl normalization ===
Input:  { baseURL: 'http://127.0.0.1:11434/v1' }
Output: baseUrl = "http://127.0.0.1:11434/v1"
PASS: true

=== Test 2: baseUrl already set → baseURL ignored ===
Input:  baseUrl='https://my-proxy.example.com/v1', baseURL='http://ignore-me.local:11434/v1'
Output: baseUrl = "https://my-proxy.example.com/v1"
PASS: true

=== Test 3: Memory search routing ===
Provider 'openai' with custom baseURL:
  embedding provider: skipped (custom endpoint)
PASS: true (built-in adapter skipped)

✅ All 3 real-behavior checks pass

$ pnpm test src/agents/memory-search.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/agents/memory-search.test.ts (37 tests)
 ✓ |runtime-config| src/config/schema.test.ts (57 tests)

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  94 passed (94)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: `baseURL` passes `.strict()` validation. The existing `superRefine` normalizes it to `baseUrl` in-place, so all downstream call sites (memory search, provider config, embedding client, remote client, gateway `/v1/embeddings`) work without changes — they already read `baseUrl`. URL redaction also works because `baseUrl` is already in the sensitive URL path list.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end with a running Ollama instance serving embeddings. Schema normalization and routing logic are independently verified.

## Tests and validation

```bash
pnpm test src/agents/memory-search.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts
# Test Files  2 passed (2)
#      Tests  94 passed (94)
```

*AI-assisted: built with Claude Code*

target.title: fix(feishu,browser,msteams,azure-speech,bedrock-mantle,googlechat,huggingface,perplexity): bound JSON response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Replace unbounded `await response.json()` with `readResponseWithLimit` (1 MiB cap)
across 8 channel/provider modules (15 call sites in 15 files).

## Changes

Modules: feishu, browser, msteams, azure-speech, bedrock-mantle, googlechat,
huggingface, perplexity. Each site uses a file-local constant.

Also fixed 15 missing constant definitions and 2 missing imports.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Untrusted API responses must not be buffered whole;
  the read must stop at the 1 MiB cap, cancel the stream, and throw a bounded
  error — while valid small responses still parse unchanged.
- **Real environment tested**: A real `node:http` server streaming
  Content-Length-less JSON bodies. Real `fetch()`, real TCP socket, real
  `readResponseWithLimit`. Node v22.12.0.

## Evidence
### Real HTTP proof (against live server)

```
node --import tsx test/_proof_bounded_json.mts

[proof] real server, cap=1048576 bytes, would-stream≈67108864 bytes

PASS  oversized body: throws bounded cap error :: msg="Error: JSON response exceeds 1048576 bytes"
PASS  stream cancelled at cap: server sent ≈ cap bytes, not ~64 MiB :: bytesSent=1048576
PASS  happy path: small JSON parsed correctly :: status=ok id=batch-1
PASS  negative control: unbounded read buffers PAST cap :: buffered=33554457 bytes (> 1048576)

[proof] 4 PASS, 0 FAIL
```

The proof harness starts a real `node:http` server with no Content-Length,
streams ~64 MiB of JSON, and verifies `readResponseWithLimit` cancels the
stream at the 1 MiB cap. The negative control proves the cap is load-bearing.

```
pnpm exec vitest run extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  17 passed (17)
```

**Label**: security

_AI-assisted._

commands.author: lsr911

target.title: fix(minimax): bound JSON response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/minimax/` providers make unbounded `await response.json()` reads
of external MiniMax API responses. A misconfigured / hostile / SSRF-reachable
endpoint can return a Content-Length-less body that streams forever — each
unbounded read buffers the entire payload before acting on it, driving the
process into memory pressure / OOM. This is the same untrusted-body surface
closed by the merged response-limit campaign (#95218 / #96027 / #96038).

Six reads are affected across 4 files: video-generation (poll response,
metadata response, create response), image-generation, OAuth token exchange,
and TTS voice list.

## Changes

Replace unbounded `await response.json()` with `readResponseWithLimit`
(1 MiB cap) in all 6 call sites. Each site uses a file-local constant
(`*_JSON_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES`) and a descriptive overflow error message.
No new abstraction — reuses the shared `media-core` bounded reader already
used across the codebase.

| File | Sites |
|------|------:|
| `video-generation-provider.ts` | 3 |
| `image-generation-provider.ts` | 1 |
| `oauth.ts` | 1 |
| `tts.ts` | 1 |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: An untrusted MiniMax API response with no
  Content-Length and an oversized / never-ending body must not be buffered
  whole; the read must stop at the 1 MiB cap, cancel the stream, and throw a
  bounded error — while valid small responses still parse unchanged.
- **Real environment tested**: A real local `node:http` server
  (`http.createServer`) bound to `127.0.0.1`, streaming a
  Content-Length-less JSON body in 64 KiB chunks (advertised ~64 MiB, well
  above the 1 MiB cap). The real `readResponseWithLimit` from the
  production `media-core` package was driven against it — real `fetch()`,
  real TCP socket, real `Response`, real bounded reader. Not an in-process
  mock. Node v22.12.0.
- **Exact steps**: `node --import tsx test/_proof_minimax_bound.mts` — start
  the server, drive `readResponseWithLimit` against the streaming overflow
  route and assert it throws + the server observed the connection was
  cancelled at ~1 MiB (not the full ~64 MiB) → run a negative control
  (unbounded `res.arrayBuffer()` on the same endpoint — buffers well past
  the cap) → drive `readResponseWithLimit` against the small valid route
  and assert it still parses.
- **What was not tested**: Did not hit the live `api.minimax.io` endpoint
  (no key / would not reproduce a hostile oversized body); the untrusted-body
  behavior is fully reproduced with a local streaming server over the same
  transport.

## Evidence

```
node --import tsx test/_proof_minimax_bound.mts

[proof] real node:http server, cap=1048576 bytes, would-stream≈67108864 bytes

PASS  oversized body: real readResponseWithLimit throws the bounded cap error :: msg="Error: MiniMax JSON response exceeds 1048576 bytes"
PASS  stream cancelled at cap: server sent ≈ cap bytes, not the full ~64 MiB body :: bytesSent=1114112 (cap=1048576, would-stream=67108864)
PASS  happy path: small valid JSON still parsed by real readResponseWithLimit :: status=ok id=batch-1
PASS  negative control: unbounded read buffers PAST the 1 MiB cap (cap is load-bearing) :: buffered=33554457 bytes (> 1048576)

[proof] 4 PASS, 0 FAIL
[proof] ALL PASS
```

In-repo Vitest suite (real ReadableStream fixtures + overflow regression test):

```
pnpm exec vitest run extensions/minimax/
  Test Files  9 passed (9)
       Tests  81 passed (81)
```

**Label**: security

_AI-assisted._

target.title: fix(memory): backfill provider.model in createWithAdapter when adapter returns empty string
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When `createWithAdapter` is called and the adapter returns an embedding
result with an empty `model` string, the search index stores the empty
string as the model identifier. This causes identity corruption in search
results where multiple embedding sources can't be distinguished.

## Changes

`extensions/memory-core/src/memory/embeddings.ts`: In `createWithAdapter`,
backfill `provider.model` from the adapter's configured model when the
returned model string is empty or undefined.

`extensions/memory-core/src/memory/embeddings.test.ts`: Test coverage
for the backfill behavior.

## Evidence

Vitest:

```
pnpm exec vitest run extensions/memory-core/src/memory/embeddings.test.ts
```

**Label**: bug

target.title: fix(docker): add optional Claude CLI install and persistent ~/.claude directory
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When running OpenClaw in Docker with `anthropic/*` providers configured to
use Claude Code (Claude Max/Pro subscription), the `@anthropic-ai/claude-code`
CLI is not installed in the container image. Users must manually rebuild the
image or exec into the container to install it. The `~/.claude` OAuth
credential directory is also not created/persisted across container upgrades.

## Changes

- Add optional `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_CLAUDE_CLI` build arg that installs
  `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` globally via npm when set (opt-in, no size
  impact for default builds)
- Create `~/.claude` directory in the Docker image with correct ownership
  so OAuth credentials survive container upgrades when backed by a volume

## Evidence

Build verification: `docker build --build-arg OPENCLAW_INSTALL_CLAUDE_CLI=1 .`
completes successfully. The default build (without the arg) is unaffected.

**Label**: enhancement
